excerpt: Intellect (al-ʿaql) is the noblest of God's gifts bestowed upon the human soul, the faculty by which it is distinguished from every animal genus and by which alone it may rise to recognize its Originator; one who does not employ his intellect toward its proper end remains in the rank of the beasts though he wear the form of a man.
source_location_hint: Opening discussions on the rank of the rational soul and the purpose of intellect

excerpt: The soul belongs to the world of the spiritual and not to the world of bodies; therefore the pleasures of the body cannot still its longing, and it remains in unrest, sighing toward its origin, until it is turned back to the source from which its light first issued.
source_authority: Bāb al-abwāb of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: Majālis on the soul's yearning for its origin and the insufficiency of bodily pleasures

excerpt: God transcends every description and every comparison; He is not to be known by what is created, for nothing stands beside Him to be compared with Him.
source_location_hint: Section on tanzīh (transcendence); doctrine of double negation, that God is neither described by attributes nor by their absence.

excerpt: The whole of the cosmos is a book of signs (āyāt) testifying to the unity of its Maker; whoever contemplates creation reads in it the imprint of tawḥīd.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the world as theatre of signs pointing to its Originator; harmonization of philosophical reason with revelation.

excerpt: The inner meanings (taʾwīl) of prayer, fasting, almsgiving, and pilgrimage are entrusted to the Imāms from the line of ʿAlī, who disclose to the qualified seeker what lies beneath the outward command.
source_authority: Chief Fatimid jurist under al-Muʿizz (4th/10th c.)
source_location_hint: Companion volume to Daʿaʾim al-Islam giving the bāṭinī exegesis of the seven pillars (ʿibādāt).

excerpt: On the inevitability of death and the soul's confession of mortality in the very phrase 'innā lillāhi wa innā ilayhi rājiʿūn — the believer's daily speech is an acknowledgement that ownership belongs to God and that perishing is the lot of the creature
source_location_hint: sayings and sermons on the certainty of death (ḥikam and khuṭab on al-mawt)

excerpt: Imam Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn's supplication at the recollection of death, in which the brevity of the world's pleasures and the equality of rich and poor before the appointed hour are pressed upon the heart of the worshipper
source_author: Imam Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn ʿAlī b. al-Ḥusayn
source_location_hint: the supplication on remembering death and the term (ajal)

excerpt: The gradation of the existents — mineral yielding itself to the plant, plant to the animal, animal to the rational soul, and the rational soul ascending toward the angelic horizon — is the visible ladder by which every rank consents to be taken up by the rank above it
source_location_hint: discussions of the order (tartīb) of being and the soul's perfection through assimilation to its superior

excerpt: The friend of God is undone by the merest spark of the divine love, for the human heart is too narrow a vessel to contain the fullness of that fire — and the lover's true station is silence and a face turned wholly toward the Beloved
source_location_hint: qaṣīdas on divine love (maḥabba) and the rapture of the awliyāʾ

excerpt: The verse 'if you love God, follow me' establishes that the love of God is verified in the believer only by adherence to the Messenger and, after him, to the appointed locus of guidance — for love of the source is shown by following the channel through which the source pours forth
source_location_hint: discourses on maḥabba and the reciprocity of love in the commentary on Q 3:31
